intro:
Philo Judaeus (Philo of Alexandria, c. 20 BC – c. AD 50) was a Jewish philosopher, biblical interpreter, and communal leader in the large Jewish community of Alexandria, Egypt. He wrote extensively in Greek, producing allegorical commentaries on the Pentateuch, philosophical treatises, apologetic works defending Judaism, and historical accounts of the Jewish community under Roman rule (including Against Flaccus and Embassy to Gaius, describing the anti-Jewish pogrom of AD 38 in Alexandria). Philo's method was to harmonize the Hebrew Scriptures with Platonic and Stoic philosophy through extensive allegorical interpretation, treating narrative and legal material as outer shells concealing deeper philosophical truths about the soul, virtue, and the divine.

Philo's concept of the Logos — the divine reason or word that mediates between the transcendent God and the material world, the image of God and the instrument of creation — is of particular interest for NT Christology. Whether the author of John's Gospel directly knew Philo's works is debated, but both drew on a common Hellenistic-Jewish intellectual environment in which the Logos concept bridged monotheistic theology and philosophical accounts of divine action. Philo identified the Logos as a 'second God' and personified divine Wisdom, though for him the Logos remained abstract and impersonal. The Christian claim that 'the Word became flesh' (John 1:14) represented a decisive departure from Philo's framework: the divine Logos was not a philosophical principle but a historical person. Philo's work was preserved almost entirely by the early church, which found his allegorical method useful even while rejecting his anti-historical tendency.
